quotes.quote: I am beyond frustrated with this game. After only playing around 6 years I had half of my rounds in the 70s and once shot 6 under on 9 holes but had to quit due to darkness. Anyway a lot of low 80s, some 70's and even a few triple digit rounds later...I want to throw my clubs in a lake, jump in drag them back out and let them melt in some meth lab explosion in the hills of Tennessee.
WTF, I hate this game, I love this game...I need help, advice, a psychoanalyst.

I cursed my clubs and walked to the range. Hit 9 irons hi, short, and to the right. Would hit one out of 3 good within 5 yards of the target. Tendency was a weak slap to the right. Practiced with my trusty 53 at 50 yd target. Pulls and shanks. Almost broke my club over my knee.
Cause I hate my driver.

The biggest thing I don't understand is one day I can smash drives out of sight, and the next day I'm struggling to get over 250, one shot I can flag a lob wedge from 50 yards and then shank 10 in a row, backwards and to the right...backwards and to the right.

It's as if I had never swung a club before. But I know tomorrow, I'll be smashing it and capable of shooting a 65 only to double bogey the last 5 holes for a 78. Dr. Bob Rotella and Sean Foley couldn't straighten out my brain or swing path.

I really want to be a scratch or plus golfer. I have the time invested but something that should have clicked years ago hasn't. Today, 23 years after my first swing...around a million shots I would guestimate...it's as if I had never swung a club before.

Every shot was a guess. I know my tendency is a little over the top and too upright...this happens when things just feel completely out of sync, and I feel this need to swing harder to live up to my standards of distance and feel.
It felt just like I was holding a moving object in my hand and I had no feel.

It's as if a swing thought works for me for about 3 days then my swing crashes completely, to where I quit and don't want to even look at my clubs...then I take a day off, go back and hit mediocre for a day, then 3 more days of good swing then crash.
My longest break due to 'I hate this game and I'm going to quit' lasted 5 weeks and that was back in 2000 or 2001.

I don't miss the resentment, negativity and one-upmanship that I personally saw at every turn when playing the game. I don't miss the grumpy old bastards complaining about slow play all the time, or upturned rakes, or divots on the fairway when golf is meant to be a hobby pursued for happiness.
I don't miss the alpha male pissing contests about how far you can hit the ball.
I used to let bad round ruin my whole day to an extent, no matter the self-talk I gave myself. And, again, the nature of golf means you're going to have many more mediocre rounds than great rounds.
Overall, I can look back now and see that having a break from the game was something that needed to happen for me. My obsession was unhealthy.
I love/hate this game of golf. Being a high handicap player that finally decided to take lessons recently the frustration is real.

Yep, I've been there before and I am there now. The last 4 or 5 times I've played, I haven't been able to really focus or care about my swing/game, which of course results in hitting a lot of bad shots and inconsistencies. And the worse you play, the greater the feeling of ambivalence.
I'm bored with long rounds and frustrated with bad results.
I found I was getting stressed out by being a slave to my index (I had a bad round, my index will go up. the horror).
I have had similar feelings but it is mainly due to frustrating play. I finally had address a couple of injuries that have been holding me back.

When I was a beginner and couldn't even break 100, one time someone who I was paired up with was like, 'Don't you keep a scorecard?'. I said it was bad enough hitting some of the shots I was hitting, why would I want to write them down and stick them in my pocket? I'd rather forget it and move.
If my rounds of golf routinely took 4-5 hours I wouldn't be in a funk for long. I'd find another pastime, maybe watching grass grow or paint dry?
I can't play golf and enjoy it if I'm standing around waiting between shots all day long. A five-hour round is roughly 2/3 standing around and 1/3 playing golf. No flow or rhythm.

Covid did the same for me. Instead of playing more like all the ones who took a couple years off. I got to play less because the courses are full. So I went from 3 rounds a week to 1 if I'm lucky... It's depressing. And my game is in the toilet.

If I'm not in a good place work wise. It's a waste of time. May as well not play. I don't know what the answer is because there's no visible light ahead economy wise, which is what will relieve some pressure on me work wise... It's probably time to pack it in and wait.

Likewise, when I get 'bored' of golf it's almost always because something's bothering me. I'm too caught up in scoring expectations or I have a nagging minor injury or something in Real Life is bumming me out and keeping me from enjoying golf.
I only get bored of telling myself I ought to score better or some expectation like that.

Golf is a luxury. Golf is a leisure activity. It's basically a mini-vacation. And nobody enjoys their vacation when they're worried about peripheral stuff like work, their relationship, family issues, or whatever distraction might be overwhelming them.
You would think that golf would be good for getting outdoors and blowing off some steam when things get hectic but I find it's actually not that way at all.
When work gets busy and my thoughts are consumed by some project, I lose my ability to focus on (and therefore enjoy) golf. It becomes one big waste of what would otherwise have been valuable time.

My present desire to step away is motivated strongly by looking at my finances and being rather disgusted by my golf spending paired with a general sense that I'm being robbed of a lot of free time I could use to accomplish more than whatever I'm accomplishing by playing golf.
I am waiting on a miracle, I really think that one day it will click and those bad swings will have worked their way out of my system.
I never take a practice swing, on the range or course. I am the most impatient person ever so that is very contradictive to the sport of golf.
I think many of us have a love hate relationship with golf.
I can definitely appreciate how an obsession of the game in various aspects could create disdain for the game.

Over the past few months I've started to notice I'm becoming a bit less interested in golf and I'm actively trying to do things, challenges for myself, YouTube series of videos, and other things to try and hold onto this one.
I do think being engaged in this community here on THP and the people I've met and communicate with here is probably the only thing keeping me even marginally interested anymore.
I stopped keeping a handicap a few years ago. Best decision I made. I play golf for the smiles now, not about the handicap revision day.
I quit a while back and when I returned there was an effort to just enjoy getting out and swinging the club. Unfortunately, the desire to improve could not be suppressed.
I had a bit of a mid-life crisis last year and decided i needed to commit to improving myself physically. i had to choose between gym and golf, and honestly i didn't miss golf that much.

My typical round will have 10 holes or so at or below par, and the other holes...10 over or worse. I can work the ball, somedays I hit shots that give me so much confidence; a hole in one, countless eagles, countless 10s.
I once had the best round of my life, every GIR plus every par 5 in 2...45 putts, YES an 82 that should have been a 62.
I obviously have a brain that gets in the way but I don't know what to do about it.
I hate this game... I hate this game... I hate this game...

People get frustrated and frankly jealous because there isn't really an almost guaranteed path to improvement through practice and playing more. Unlike running - you run more your fitness improves, provided you recover properly.

I enjoy seeing the objective improvement that correlates strongly to effort that running gives. Being able to run a little further, or a bit quicker gives a wonderful sense of achievement. Again, the nature of golf means improvement is rarely so linear.
Hit 9 irons hi, short, and to the right. Would hit one out of 3 good within 5 yards of the target. Tendency was a weak slap to the right.
50% of my 58 and 53 degree wedges flew on a sour, boomerangish, right destined path.
